topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a finality that felt heavier than any closing of a boardroom. Elias stood in the echoing silence of the empty office, the scent of old paper and expensive leather suddenly suffocating. He reread the framed photograph on the desk—a smiling, younger version of his mentor, Arthur, standing beside a bustling project team. Arthur was gone. No note. No farewell. Just an abrupt cessation of presence. Elias traced the outline of the photograph with a trembling finger, the fine hairs on his arms prickling. He felt a knot tighten in his chest, a cold, hard pressure that made drawing a full breath a conscious effort. He started pacing the perimeter of the room, the sound of his own footsteps too loud in the stillness. He had been so focused on the next milestone, on the metrics, that he hadn't noticed the subtle shift in Arthur’s demeanor, the quiet withdrawal. Now, the weight of unspoken words settled on him, a leaden cloak he couldn't shrug off.

[story 2]

"Arthur?" The name sounded hollow, swallowed instantly by the vast, unused space of the study. Sarah clutched the lukewarm coffee mug so tightly her knuckles were white. The silence that followed the phone call, the abrupt silence after Arthur’s last, clipped sentence confirming his retirement, was deafening. She sank onto the plush visitor's chair, the velvet sighing beneath her weight. Her gaze kept drifting to the framed commendation on the mantelpiece, a testament to a career she had so deeply admired. A sharp, unpleasant churning began low in her stomach, a sensation like bitter acid rising. She picked up her phone, her thumb hovering over Arthur's contact, then stopping. What would she even say? The words felt clumsy, inadequate, like trying to capture a vast ocean in a teacup. She let out a slow, ragged exhale, the air catching painfully in her throat. She stood up, moving toward the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling utterly adrift and profoundly ungrateful for the years spent under his tutelage.

[story 3]

Mark sat at his desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in his tired eyes. The resignation email had arrived this morning, polite and brief, confirming Arthur's departure from the firm. Mark stared at the screen, the text blurring into meaningless characters. He picked up the heavy, silver pen from his drawer, turning it over and over until the metal felt cold against his palm. A deep sense of inadequacy washed over him, a wave of self-reproach so intense it made his jaw ache. He remembered the late nights, the hours he’d spent agonizing over minor details, all under Arthur’s patient, knowing gaze. He wanted to reach out, to apologize for not having seen the larger picture, to somehow salvage the final moments. Instead, he just leaned back in his chair, the springs groaning under his weight, and stared blankly at the wall. Every muscle in his body felt tense, coiled tight, as if bracing for an impact that hadn't yet arrived.
